storyDialogue.text: Platform 7, Shinjuku Station. The departure board flickered — 14:55 to Kyoto. Delayed by twelve minutes. Enough time to ruin everything, or fix it.
Three suitcases. I brought three suitcases to leave a city I've lived in for twenty-two years, and somehow I still feel like I'm forgetting something important.
My mother would say I'm overthinking it. She always says that. Usually while overthinking her own problems with twice the intensity.
A voice cut through the station noise — familiar, breathless, and entirely unexpected.
Hana! Wait — just — hold on, I ran from Takadanobaba, give me a second—
Kaito?! What are you doing here? I told you not to come.
You told me not to come to the farewell party. You didn't say anything about the train station. Loophole.
That's not a loophole. That's a deliberate misunderstanding.
My specialty.
You're sweating through your jacket.
It's October and I sprinted two kilometers. What did you expect, a magazine cover?
I expected you to respect my clearly stated boundary about goodbyes.
I do respect it. I'm not here to say goodbye. I'm here to give you something.
He reached into his messenger bag — the one with the broken zipper he'd been meaning to replace since university — and pulled out a shoebox.
...Did you bring me shoes?
What? No. It's the only box I had. Open it.
Inside the shoebox, wrapped in tissue paper that had clearly been crumpled, smoothed, and recrumpled at least twice: letters. Dozens of them. Handwritten, dated, folded into uneven thirds.
What is this?
Letters. One for every month you'll be in Kyoto. Twelve months, twelve letters. I numbered them. Read them in order or the narrative doesn't work.
You wrote me twelve letters.
Technically fourteen. I messed up February twice and March once. The ones in there are the final drafts.
Kaito, you once told me you hadn't handwritten anything since your grandmother's birthday card in 2019.
And my handwriting has not improved since then. You'll notice.
Why letters? You could have texted. Called. Sent voice messages like a normal person in the twenty-first century.

Because texts disappear into the scroll. Calls fade from memory. But letters — you can hold a letter. You can put it on a desk and look at it and know that someone sat down for an hour with terrible penmanship and chose every word on purpose.
I wanted you to have something that couldn't be deleted.
The departure board updated. 14:55 to Kyoto — now boarding, Platform 7. The twelve-minute delay had expired.
I have to go.
I know.
I said no goodbyes.
And I said I'm not here to say goodbye. I'm here to say: read the March letter last. I know it's number three. Read it last anyway.
That defeats the entire purpose of numbering them.
Trust me.
Kaito.
Yeah?
Thank you. For the loophole.
Anytime. Now go — you're going to miss your train trying to be poetic.
She picked up all three suitcases. One in each hand, one under her arm. It was absurd and graceless and entirely Hana.
She didn't look back. He didn't expect her to. That was the deal they'd never made but always understood.
On the train, somewhere past Shinagawa, she opened the shoebox. She read January first. Then February. Then skipped to April, as instructed.
She saved March for Kyoto. For a rainy evening, six months later, when missing someone had become a background noise she'd learned to live with.
The March letter was four words long. She read it eleven times.
